text: When the sun shone, it lighted the whole place.

"permanent positive attitude" is the object. It looks hard because it is a long sentence, but it is much simpler theoretically.

text: The only thing that is truly ours, The only thing that no one can take away from us, is our attitude.

Commas would be correct if it was an adverbial that is movable around the sentence (like "of course", "according to Jerry", "that we can tell from this story"). Em dash is more radical than comma. Both are equally correct but for different occasions. Em dash follows the format "IC -- thought -- IC".

text: * The Third Rule: Experiences don't lead to mistakes, but they lead to lessons learned.

Commas with conjunctions separate two independent clauses (like this sentence: take out ",but" and it is two complete sentences). But if the two clauses have the same verb like "lead", you can just shorten the second clause like this so that it goes into the first. So lead refers to not making mistakes but also to the lessons learned.

text: Wherever love, there are wealth and success.
correct: Wherever one has love, there is wealth and success. Whenever talking about singular concepts like "wealth", say "there is". Like "There is wealth in this town." You'd also say it for "There is a dog in this town." Singular goes with "is." "There are" is for things or lists of things with plural items. Like "There are hotels in this town." Or "There are five cats, two dogs, and a rabbit." If the list is mixed look to the first item in the list. "There are six dogs, a cat, and a rabbit." And "There is a rabbit, two dogs, and a cat." But as long as all the items in the list are singular, it should always get "is". Such as "There is a dog, a cat, and a rabbit in here." Or for concepts without "a": "There is wealth, success, and love in this story."

If you are writing one of these words in a sentence, ask yourself if you are creating a dependent clause and thus need a comma. This first part of this sentence is independent and the second part is a dependent. And both sections of the sentence can be reversed... meaning it is IC, DC.

Semi-colon should only be used for two reasons: 1) connecting two independent clauses such as "He went back to the grocery store; milk is what he forgot." 2) acting as a comma if commas are part of things in a list (called a "super comma") such as "This summer, I will visit Barcelona, Spain; Paris, France; and Milan, Italy." In that case it is just part of title (the city, country) so to avoid confusion one uses semi-colon. The funny thing is that most English writers do not know how to use the semi-colon (or em dash "--" for that matter), but you do now!

text: I call him professor Okay, because he seems not capable of completing one sentence without using the word okay 2 or 3 times.

Usually they have earned the title... like with a PhD or by working at a university, but it also works in jokes, as well, by analogy. Especially if he prefers his real title like Professor Smith or whatever his real name is. Just because it is like a nickname... and nicknames would be capitalized, because it is technically something you might address them as (even if not to their face).

For Americans, "bloody" means simply covered in blood. "He had a bloody gash on his leg after the car accident". For British, it's an intensifier that can be used in pretty much any situation that Americans (at least, Americans who swear) might use "fucking" or "damn/ed". ("That damned dog won't stop barking!) text: "Stay in home" is more physical.

You cannot do that in the second sentence. I typically think of "in" as being "inside" the location you are talking about. And "at" is attached to the location that you are doing the verb. text: I enjoyed talking to local people who are friends of hers and I could get to know a lot about Hawaii thanks to them.
correct: I enjoyed talking to local people who are friends of hers and I was able to learn a lot about Hawaii thanks to them. Comment: In English, we usually "learn about" things rather than "get to know" things. The only exception I can think of at the moment is when the object is a person. Then either works, but they mean slightly different things. When you "get to know" a person, then you are personally familiar with them. When you "learn about" a person, then you have gained information describing the person. While in principle I suppose the same could apply to a place (such as Hawaii), I have never heard it in practice.

text: Today, it was very cold and strong windy day in Tokyo.
correct: Today, it was very cold and windy day in Tokyo. When you put a -y at the end of weather words (wind-y, snow-y, storm-y, etc.) the words become an adjective; a word used to describe nouns. Strong is also an adjective. You want to say the wind was strong but, when you put one adjective in front of another "strong windy" they both describe the next noun ---> day. What happens is you have a cold day in Tokyo. You have a windy day in Tokyo. Finally you have a strong day in Tokyo!? If you want to talk about how windy the day was you can use the word very to talk about how the windy the wind was. It was very cold and there was a very strong wind today in Tokyo!

text: what is the difference between Tell off and scold?
correct: what is the difference between Tell off and scold? "Tell off" and "scold" are near synonyms (same meaning), but to "tell someone off" generally means an angry, personal, rebuke -- often defensive against a real or imagined insult. "scold" is often used to refer to the rebuke a parent gives to a child for bad behavior. "Scold" is briefly irritated and might be angry, but "tell off" is always angry, often unfocussed. that is, you might tell someone off about a wide variety of topics.

Knowing when to use 'a' and when to use 'the' can be quite difficult, and as a native English speaker it's hard for me to remember the exact grammatical rules that may apply to this situation, but I will give as much advice as I can.Using 'a' is common when speaking of a single object, or one of many. "I read a newspaper." or "I read a NHK business English textbook." In the second example, it sounds as if there are many NHK business textbooks and you have read one of them. If there is one commonly known NHK textbook, then I suppose 'the' could be used. 'The' is often placed in front of titles and more specific media. For example, "I read the Japan Times." Even though the name is actually, "The Japan Times", you wouldn't say, "I read the The Japan Times." English just cuts one of those out. Since this probably didn't answer your question very well, I'll try to write some more examples to help you find a pattern for yourself. Here on Lang-8 we don't (yet) have the luxury of what are called "en dashes" or "em dashes" (indicating the width of our letters N and M). So, double hyphens, IMHO, with spaces on either side indicating dashes, which are something like parentheses, are the clearest way to go. Example: "I was talking to my wife the other night -- baseball season being over -- and she said .............." It's kind of like what we call an "aside," sort of like when an actor turns toward the audience with a little quip: -- baseball season being over --. ;-) ]

Both fit in this sentence, but "dropping by [a place]" suggests that you go inside and stay for a bit, while "stopping by" simply means you stopped real quick to do something. That's why you "stop by the office" to quickly pick up some things, but you "drop by a friend" to have a cup of tea and chat for a bit.)
